plainMeaning: This is the twelfth lay vow — sharing your own pure food with Jain monks — and here you confess five sly ways of giving badly: stashing the food on or under living things so the monk must refuse, offering it at a time he cannot accept, calling your own thing too precious or someone else's, and giving grudgingly, out of rivalry, or stingily.
recitation: 12. Bāramuṁ atithi saṁvibhāga vrata. Jaṁ parvataṇe pāraṇe sādhune śuddhamāna dāna dei āpaṇāpuṁ upajīvīe, te atithi saṁvibhāga vrata kahīe. E bāramā atithi saṁvibhāga vratataṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. Sacitta nikkhevaṇayā, sacitta pihaṇayā, kālāikkamadāṇe, parovaese, macchharayā. Sūjatuṁ annapānādika aṇadevānī buddhe sacitta upare mūkyuṁ hoya athavā sacitte ḍhāṅkyuṁ hoya. Kālātikrama:— veḷā ṭāḷī annapānataṇī nimantraṇā kīdhī hoya. Paravyapadeśa:— āpaṇī vastu piyārī kahī hoya. Macchhara-lage anerātaṇuṁ dāna dekhī tenī spardhāe dāna dīdhuṁ hoya athavā dānataṇī veḷāe mahātmā, tumheṁ amāruṁ kāṁī leśo to tumaśuṁ amāruṁ rūṣaṇuṁ che, em kahī kalaha māṇḍyo hoya; tathā glānādika kāraṇa pākhe asūjhatuṁ dīdhuṁ hoya, sūjatuṁ kṛpaṇapaṇe dīdhuṁ na hoya. Aneruṁ e bāramā atithi saṁvibhāga vrata viṣe.

literalTranslation: Twelfth, the Guest-Sharing vow. That whereby, at the fast-breaking of a holy day, having given pure, fitting alms to a monk, we then sustain ourselves by our own means — that is called the Guest-Sharing vow. I now scrutinise the five transgressions of this twelfth Guest-Sharing vow: placing-upon living matter, covering-with living matter, giving past the time, attributing-to-another, and envy. (1–2) With the intent of not giving, acceptable food, drink and the like may have been set upon something living, or covered with something living. (3) Time-transgression: letting the (alms) hour pass, an invitation for food and drink may have been made. (4) Attributing-to-another: one's own thing may have been called 'too dear (to give).' (5) Out of envy, seeing another's gift, one may have given in rivalry with it; or, at the time of giving, picking a quarrel by saying, 'Reverend sir, if you take anything of ours, then we have a grudge with you'; and, absent a cause such as a monk's illness, unfit food may have been given, while fit food may not have been given, or given only stingily. And whatever else (was done) concerning this twelfth Guest-Sharing vow.
idiomaticTranslation: The twelfth is the vow of sharing with the worthy guest. When, on a holy day's fast-breaking, we first set pure and faultless alms before a monk and only then nourish ourselves — that is the Guest-Sharing vow. Let me now cleanse its five transgressions: setting the food on living things, covering it with living things, offering it after the right hour, disowning it as another's, and giving in envy. Scheming not to give, I may have laid fit food upon something living, or hidden it beneath something living. I may have let the alms-hour slip by and only then 'invited' the monk to food and drink. I may have pleaded that my own thing was too precious to part with. Out of jealousy I may have given merely to outdo another's gift; or, at the very moment of giving, I may have provoked a quarrel — 'Reverend one, if you accept anything of ours, we shall hold it against you!' — and, with no excusing cause such as a monk's sickness, I may have handed over unacceptable food while withholding the acceptable, or doled it out like a miser. And whatever else I have done amiss touching this twelfth vow.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This block closes the four śikṣāvratas by turning the householder's table into a field of merit. Its premise — given before the faults — is exact: alms are offered first to the monk, and only the remainder feeds the family ('āpaṇāpuṁ upajīvīe'). The five transgressions are not failures to give but corruptions of the *manner* of giving, and the text exposes their shared root in a single phrase, 'aṇadevānī buddhe' — 'with the intent of not-giving.' The first pair, sacitta-nikṣepa and sacitta-pidhāna, is the subtlest hypocrisy in the whole sūtra: the donor keeps perfectly acceptable food, but deliberately rests it upon, or buries it under, living matter (a heap of grain, a green leaf, water) so that the monk's own eṣaṇā-rules compel him to refuse — a refusal the donor secretly wants. One thereby *appears* generous while engineering a decline. The third, kālātikrama, works the same trick through timing: extend the invitation only once the monk's alms-round is over, so the offer is safely empty. The fourth, paravyapadeśa, evades by speech — here vividly rendered as calling one's own givable thing 'piyārī,' too dear to part with (the classical form disowns it as belonging to another). The fifth, mātsarya, is laid out most fully: giving merely to *outdo* a rival donor; and worse, manufacturing a quarrel at the moment of offering — 'if you take anything of ours we will resent you' — so the monk withdraws. The verse then appends two further mean-spirited slips: giving unfit (asūjhatuṁ) food where no excusing cause such as a monk's illness ('glānādik') exists, and giving fit food only 'kṛpaṇapaṇe,' like a miser. Each is sealed by the confessional 'hoya,' and the block ends in the standard catch-all 'aneruṁ… viṣe.'
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The vow rests on supātra-dāna — giving to a worthy vessel — the layperson's principal channel for puṇya and for sustaining the saṅgha that the householder cannot yet join. Doctrinally the passage teaches that dāna is judged by bhāva, not by the transfer of an object: the act can be outwardly complete yet inwardly void when poisoned by mātsarya or by the will-not-to-give. Two Jain disciplines intersect here. First, ahiṁsā and the monk's eṣaṇā-samiti: food touching sacitta becomes unaccceptable because accepting it implicates the monk in harm to one-sensed beings — which is precisely why a cunning donor abuses sacitta as a tool of refusal. Second, the saṁlekhanā-ward arc of the lay path: by repeatedly placing the guest first, the śrāvaka rehearses the aparigraha and detachment that the next (and final) stage of the text, the death-fast, will demand. The single sanctioned exception — unfit food permitted only for a sick monk ('glānādik kāraṇ') — shows the Jain ethic of apavāda: a rule may bend for compassion, never for convenience.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When you give — to monastics, to the needy, to any worthy cause — examine the intent the verse exposes: are you arranging excuses so the offer quietly fails? Give the good thing, not the leftover; give on time, not after the chance has passed; never give to outshine a neighbour or while nursing a grudge, and never let a 'gift' become an occasion for a quarrel or a condition. Withhold only genuine help, and give it ungrudgingly, in full measure.

sources.title: Twelve Vows of Lay People (Romanized)
sources.consultedFor: Placement of Atithi Saṁvibhāga as the 12th vow among the four śikṣāvratas, and the pratikramaṇa-atichāra framing.

sources.title: Atithisaṃvibhāga — definition (Jainism, Tattvārthasūtra & Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra)
sources.consultedFor: Definition of the vow (fourfold giving with a pure heart) and corroboration of its Tattvārtha-sūtra source and atichāra scheme.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim Gujarati text of the verse and its book-specific orthography (atiyāra with ય).

notes: The five atichāra-names match the canonical list (Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.31 Śvet. / 7.21 Dig.; numbering varies by recension): sacitta-nikṣepa, sacitta-pidhāna, kālātikrama, para-vyapadeśa, mātsarya. The verse here orders them nikṣepa–pidhāna–kālātikrama–para-vyapadeśa–mātsarya, with para-vyapadeśa given the Gujarati turn 'āpaṇī vastu piyārī kahī' ('called one's own item too dear/another's') rather than the strict classical 'designating one's own as belonging to another'; both senses are intended and noted. 'śuddhamāna' is read as 'pure-and-fitting (eṣaṇīya)' alms (= sūjatuṁ); a minority reading takes -māna as 'with due respect/measure.' Readings (including atiyāra with ય) follow the source book verbatim and are not normalised. Web verification confirmed the vow's identity, definition and atichāra scheme; the exact printed page was not independently re-imaged in this session.
